Description: Functional components are the modern standard in React Native and provide better performance and cleaner code than class components
Do: Write functional components using useState and useEffect hooks
Don't: Use class components for new code
Code Good: const MyComponent = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return <Text>{count}</Text>; }
Code Bad: class MyComponent extends Component { state = { count: 0 }; render() { return <Text>{this.state.count}</Text>; } }

Description: Use React.memo to prevent unnecessary re-renders of components that receive the same props
Do: Wrap pure components with React.memo when they render frequently
Don't: Memoize every component without profiling first
Code Good: const ExpensiveList = React.memo(({ items }) => { return items.map(item => <Item key={item.id} {...item} />); });
Code Bad: const ExpensiveList = ({ items }) => { return items.map(item => <Item key={item.id} {...item} />); };

Description: Break down complex UIs into smaller reusable components for better maintainability and testing
Do: Create atomic components that handle one responsibility
Don't: Build monolithic components with multiple concerns
Code Good: const Button = ({ title, onPress, variant }) => <Pressable onPress={onPress}><Text>{title}</Text></Pressable>;
Code Bad: const Screen = () => { /* 500 lines of mixed UI logic */ };

Description: Component names should be descriptive PascalCase and reflect their purpose
Do: Name components based on their function like UserProfileCard
Don't: Use generic names like Component1 or MyView
Code Good: export const TraderRankingCard = ({ trader }) => { ... };

Description: StyleSheet.create optimizes styles and validates them at compile time providing better performance
Do: Define styles using StyleSheet.create outside component body
Don't: Define inline style objects repeatedly
Code Good: const styles = StyleSheet.create({ container: { flex: 1, padding: 16 } });

Description: Inline styles create new objects on every render causing unnecessary reconciliation work
Do: Use pre-defined styles from StyleSheet or cached style references
Don't: Pass object literals directly to style prop
Code Good: <View style={styles.container}>

Description: React Native uses Flexbox for layout which works consistently across platforms
Do: Master flexDirection alignItems justifyContent and flex properties
Don't: Use absolute positioning for everything
Code Good: const styles = StyleSheet.create({ row: { flexDirection: 'row', justifyContent: 'space-between' } });
Code Bad: const styles = StyleSheet.create({ item: { position: 'absolute', top: 100, left: 50 } });

Description: Support different screen sizes using Dimensions API or useWindowDimensions hook
Do: Calculate sizes relative to screen dimensions or use percentage values
Don't: Hardcode pixel values that only work on one device size
Code Good: const { width } = useWindowDimensions(); const cardWidth = width * 0.9;

Description: Apply different styles for iOS and Android when needed using Platform module
Do: Use Platform.select or Platform.OS for platform-specific styling
Don't: Write separate stylesheets for each platform
Code Good: const styles = StyleSheet.create({ shadow: Platform.select({ ios: { shadowColor: '#000' }, android: { elevation: 4 } }) });
Code Bad: // ios-styles.js and android-styles.js with duplicated code

Description: React Navigation is the recommended navigation solution with excellent performance and customization
Do: Install and configure React Navigation with proper TypeScript types
Don't: Build custom navigation from scratch
Code Good: const Stack = createNativeStackNavigator(); <Stack.Navigator><Stack.Screen name='Home' component={Home} /></Stack.Navigator>

Description: TypeScript typing for navigation prevents runtime errors and improves developer experience
Do: Define RootStackParamList and use typed hooks like useNavigation<NavigationProp>
Don't: Use any type or skip navigation typing
Code Good: type Props = NativeStackScreenProps<RootStackParamList, 'Profile'>; const ProfileScreen = ({ navigation, route }: Props) => { ... };

Description: Configure deep linking for better user experience and marketing campaign support
Do: Set up linking configuration with proper path patterns
Don't: Ignore deep linking requirements until launch
Code Good: const linking = { prefixes: ['myapp://'], config: { screens: { Home: '', Profile: 'user/:id' } } };

Description: Save and restore navigation state for better UX when app is backgrounded
Do: Implement onStateChange and initialState for navigation container
Don't: Let navigation reset on every app restart
Code Good: <NavigationContainer onStateChange={state => saveState(state)} initialState={savedState}>

Description: Choose the right state management solution based on complexity and team familiarity
Do: Use Context for simple global state and Zustand/Redux for complex state
Don't: Put all state in Redux even for simple local state
Code Good: const useTraderStore = create((set) => ({ traders: [], fetchTraders: async () => { ... } }));
Code Bad: const store = createStore(rootReducer); // For a simple counter

Description: Place state at the lowest common ancestor that needs it to minimize re-renders
Do: Keep state close to where it is used and lift only when necessary
Don't: Store everything in global state for convenience
Code Good: const Parent = () => { const [filter, setFilter] = useState(''); return <SearchBar value={filter} onChange={setFilter} />; };
Code Bad: const globalState = { searchFilter: '', userName: '', theme: '' }; // All in one place

Description: useReducer provides predictable state updates for complex state logic with multiple sub-values
Do: Use useReducer when state logic is complex or involves multiple related values
Don't: Use multiple useState calls for tightly related state
Code Good: const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0, step: 1 });
Code Bad: const [count, setCount] = useState(0); const [step, setStep] = useState(1); const [min, setMin] = useState(0);

Description: Pass data through context or state management instead of through many component layers
Do: Use Context or state management for deeply nested data requirements
Don't: Pass props through 5+ component levels
Code Good: const ThemeContext = createContext(); const Button = () => { const theme = useContext(ThemeContext); };

Description: FlatList renders items lazily providing much better performance than ScrollView for long lists
Do: Use FlatList or SectionList for lists with more than a handful of items
Don't: Render long lists with ScrollView and map
Code Good: <FlatList data={traders} renderItem={({ item }) => <TraderCard trader={item} />} keyExtractor={item => item.id} />

Guideline: Implement getItemLayout for fixed height items
Description: getItemLayout skips measurement of items improving scroll performance significantly
Do: Provide getItemLayout when all items have the same height
Don't: Skip getItemLayout optimization for lists with many items
Code Good: <FlatList getItemLayout={(data, index) => ({ length: 80, offset: 80 * index, index })} />

Description: Unique stable keys help React Native track items efficiently during updates
Do: Use unique stable IDs as keys
Don't: Use array index as key for dynamic lists
Code Good: <FlatList keyExtractor={(item) => item.id.toString()} />

Description: Memoize renderItem to prevent unnecessary re-renders of list items
Do: Wrap renderItem in useCallback with proper dependencies
Don't: Define renderItem inline without memoization
Code Good: const renderItem = useCallback(({ item }) => <Card item={item} />, []);

Description: Load data in chunks to reduce memory usage and improve initial load time
Do: Use onEndReached for infinite scroll pagination
Don't: Load entire dataset at once
Code Good: <FlatList onEndReached={loadMore} onEndReachedThreshold={0.5} ListFooterComponent={loading && <Spinner />} />

Description: Hermes significantly improves startup time memory usage and app size
Do: Enable Hermes in your build configuration
Don't: Use JSC when Hermes is available and suitable
Code Good: // android/app/build.gradle: hermesEnabled: true

Description: Minimize re-renders by using proper memoization and state management
Do: Use React.memo useMemo and useCallback appropriately
Don't: Ignore re-render optimization until performance issues arise
Code Good: const MemoizedComponent = React.memo(Component); const value = useMemo(() => compute(a, b), [a, b]);
Code Bad: const Component = (props) => { const value = expensiveComputation(); return <View />; };

Description: Defer expensive operations until after animations complete for smoother UX
Do: Schedule heavy work with InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions
Don't: Run heavy computations during navigation transitions
Code Good: InteractionManager.runAfterInteractions(() => { processLargeDataset(); });

Description: Use React DevTools Profiler to identify performance bottlenecks
Do: Profile components to find unnecessary re-renders and slow renders
Don't: Guess at performance issues without profiling
Code Good: // Use React DevTools Profiler in development to analyze renders
Code Bad: // Just add React.memo everywhere hoping it helps

Description: react-native-fast-image provides aggressive image caching and better performance
Do: Use FastImage for images that need caching and priority loading
Don't: Use Image component for frequently loaded remote images
Code Good: import FastImage from 'react-native-fast-image'; <FastImage source={{ uri: url, priority: FastImage.priority.high }} />

Description: Load images at the size they will be displayed to reduce memory usage
Do: Resize images on the server or use resizeMode appropriately
Don't: Load full resolution images for small thumbnails
Code Good: <Image source={{ uri: thumbnailUrl }} style={{ width: 50, height: 50 }} resizeMode='cover' />
Code Bad: <Image source={{ uri: fullSizeUrl }} style={{ width: 50, height: 50 }} />

Description: Show placeholder or skeleton while images load for better perceived performance
Do: Use placeholder images or skeleton loading states
Don't: Show empty space while images load
Code Good: <FastImage source={{ uri }} defaultSource={placeholder} style={styles.image} />

Description: Cache frequently accessed images to reduce bandwidth and improve offline experience
Do: Implement image caching strategy for your use case
Don't: Re-download images on every app launch
Code Good: FastImage.preload([{ uri: 'https://example.com/logo.png' }]);
Code Bad: // No caching strategy for repeated images

Description: Controlled components give you full control over form state and validation
Do: Manage input values through state
Don't: Use uncontrolled inputs with refs for complex forms
Code Good: const [email, setEmail] = useState(''); <TextInput value={email} onChangeText={setEmail} />
Code Bad: const inputRef = useRef(); // Access value via inputRef.current.value

Description: Handle keyboard appearance to prevent it from covering inputs
Do: Use KeyboardAvoidingView and proper keyboard types
Don't: Ignore keyboard overlap issues
Code Good: <KeyboardAvoidingView behavior={Platform.OS === 'ios' ? 'padding' : 'height'}><TextInput keyboardType='email-address' /></KeyboardAvoidingView>

Description: Validate user input to prevent errors and improve data quality
Do: Implement validation with clear error messages
Don't: Submit forms without validation
Code Good: const validateEmail = (email) => /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email); {!valid && <Text style={styles.error}>Invalid email</Text>}

Description: Set the correct keyboardType for each input to improve user experience
Do: Match keyboardType to the expected input type
Don't: Use default keyboard for all inputs
Code Good: <TextInput keyboardType='numeric' /> <TextInput keyboardType='email-address' /> <TextInput keyboardType='phone-pad' />

Description: Pressable is the modern replacement for TouchableOpacity with more flexibility
Do: Use Pressable with proper feedback states
Don't: Use deprecated Touchable components for new code
Code Good: <Pressable onPress={handlePress} style={({ pressed }) => [styles.btn, pressed && styles.pressed]}><Text>Press Me</Text></Pressable>

Description: Increase touch target size for small interactive elements
Do: Add hitSlop for elements smaller than 44x44 points
Don't: Use small touch targets without hit slop
Code Good: <Pressable hitSlop={{ top: 10, bottom: 10, left: 10, right: 10 }} onPress={onPress}><Icon size={20} /></Pressable>

Description: Give users immediate visual feedback when they interact with elements
Do: Show pressed states and loading indicators
Don't: Have no visual response to touch interactions
Code Good: <Pressable style={({ pressed }) => ({ opacity: pressed ? 0.7 : 1 })}><Text>Button</Text></Pressable>

Description: Use react-native-gesture-handler for complex gestures that perform better than PanResponder
Do: Use gesture handler for swipes pinches and complex interactions
Don't: Use PanResponder for complex gesture recognition
Code Good: import { GestureDetector, Gesture } from 'react-native-gesture-handler'; const pan = Gesture.Pan().onUpdate(e => { ... });

Description: react-native-reanimated runs animations on the UI thread for 60fps performance
Do: Use Reanimated for complex or gesture-driven animations
Don't: Use Animated API for gesture-based animations
Code Good: const progress = useSharedValue(0); const style = useAnimatedStyle(() => ({ transform: [{ translateX: progress.value }] }));
Code Bad: const translateX = new Animated.Value(0); Animated.timing(translateX, { toValue: 100 }).start();

Description: Native driver runs animations on the native thread avoiding JS thread bottlenecks
Do: Set useNativeDriver true for transform and opacity animations
Don't: Run all animations on JS thread
Code Good: Animated.timing(fadeAnim, { toValue: 1, useNativeDriver: true }).start();

Description: Use LayoutAnimation for simple layout changes without manual animation code
Do: Use LayoutAnimation for list additions removals and layout changes
Don't: Manually animate every layout change
Code Good: LayoutAnimation.configureNext(LayoutAnimation.Presets.easeInEaseOut); setItems([...items, newItem]);

Description: Show appropriate loading indicators while data is being fetched
Do: Display loading state and handle errors gracefully
Don't: Show blank screen while loading
Code Good: const { data, loading, error } = useQuery(); if (loading) return <Spinner />; if (error) return <ErrorView />;

Description: Catch and handle errors from async operations to prevent crashes
Do: Use try-catch and display user-friendly error messages
Don't: Let async errors crash the app
Code Good: try { const data = await fetchData(); setData(data); } catch (error) { setError('Failed to load data'); }

Description: Prevent memory leaks and state updates on unmounted components
Do: Use AbortController or cleanup functions in useEffect
Don't: Ignore cleanup of async operations
Code Good: useEffect(() => { const controller = new AbortController(); fetch(url, { signal: controller.signal }); return () => controller.abort(); }, []);

Description: Provide meaningful labels for screen readers to describe UI elements
Do: Add accessibilityLabel to interactive and important elements
Don't: Ship without accessibility labels
Code Good: <Pressable accessibilityLabel='Submit order' accessibilityRole='button' onPress={submit}><Icon name='check' /></Pressable>

Description: Set accessibilityRole to help screen readers understand element purpose
Do: Assign appropriate roles like button link header image
Don't: Omit accessibility roles from interactive elements
Code Good: <Pressable accessibilityRole='button'><Text>Click me</Text></Pressable>

Description: Respect user font size preferences for better accessibility
Do: Use allowFontScaling and test with different text sizes
Don't: Disable font scaling or use fixed font sizes
Code Good: <Text allowFontScaling={true} maxFontSizeMultiplier={1.5}>Scalable text</Text>

Description: Test business logic and utility functions with Jest
Do: Write tests for complex functions and state management
Don't: Skip testing until bugs are reported
Code Good: test('formatCurrency', () => { expect(formatCurrency(1000)).toBe('$1,000.00'); });

Description: Test components from a user perspective focusing on behavior not implementation
Do: Write tests that interact with components like a user would
Don't: Test implementation details and internal state
Code Good: const { getByText } = render(<Button title='Submit' />); fireEvent.press(getByText('Submit'));

Description: Bridge to native code only when necessary for performance or platform features
Do: Create native modules for CPU-intensive tasks or native-only APIs
Don't: Write native code for things achievable in JS
Code Good: // Use native module for biometric auth NativeModules.BiometricAuth.authenticate();
Code Bad: // Native module for simple string formatting

Description: Expo provides a managed workflow with many built-in features and easier updates
Do: Use Expo for projects that fit within its capabilities
Don't: Use bare React Native when Expo covers your needs
Code Good: npx create-expo-app my-app; expo install expo-camera;
Code Bad: npx react-native init my-app; // Then manually configure camera
